title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
What happens when your term insurance expires?
Meta description: Is your term insurance plan close to the expiry date? Check out what happens when your term insurance expires.
Keywords: term life insurance, what is term insurance, buy term insurance
A term life insurance plan provides your family with some much-needed financial support even when you’re not there. Whatever your family’s life goals may be, an adequate term insurance plan can help satisfy them. That’s why it is absolutely essential to ensure that your term insurance remains active at all times. Letting it expire can put your family at a huge risk. Wondering what would happen if your term plan expires? Let’s take a look.
What is term insurance?
Term insurance is a contract between an individual and an insurance company, where the insurer agrees to pay out a predetermined sum of money in the event of the individual’s death. And in exchange for this benefit, the individual is required to make periodic payments, known as premiums, to the insurance company.
What happens when your term insurance expires?
Now, term life insurance is valid only for a certain period of time, known as the policy term, beyond which it automatically expires. Here’s an example to help you better understand how it works.
Let’s say that an individual buys term insurance with a death benefit sum assured of Rs. 50 lakhs for a policy term of 25 years. In exchange, the individual is required to pay Rs. 2,000 each month as premium to the insurer till the end of the policy term. And if the individual were to face an untimely death during the policy term of 25 years, his beneficiaries would receive the death benefit sum assured of Rs. 50 lakhs.
But then, what if the individual survives till the end of the policy term of 25 years? Simple. The policy expires automatically and ceases to exist. And when that happens, the individual and his family are no longer protected by the term plan and none of the benefits under the plan would be applicable to them.
Therefore, if the individual happens to face an untimely death in the year after the expiry of his term life insurance policy, his family will not receive any death benefit sum assured payouts or any of the other benefits under the plan. This leaves them unprotected from life’s uncertainties and puts them in a precarious financial position.
What to do when it expires?
So then, what should you do when your term insurance plan expires? This is a common question that many individuals have. And here’s the answer to it - buy term insurance once again. Getting a new term life insurance plan once your existing one expires is the best course of action that you can take.
That said, here’s a tip. Once your existing term plan expires, it is advisable to buy term insurance as quickly as possible. This way, you can significantly minimize the time that you leave yourself and your family unprotected.
However, before purchasing a new term plan, check the eligibility criteria first. Many insurance companies have a maximum entry age beyond which term insurance policies are not issued. If you’re worried about not being eligible to buy term insurance again on account of age, you can easily avoid this issue by opting for a whole life term insurance plan during your first purchase itself. A whole life term plan offers insurance coverage till the age of 99.
Conclusion
As you can see, a term insurance, upon expiry, leaves both the policyholder and the policyholder’s family at risk. That’s precisely why it is essential to minimize the time you leave your family unprotected. And you can do that by simply purchasing another term life insurance plan.
Wondering whether you can simply extend your policy tenure instead of having to buy term insurance all over again? Unfortunately, once you’ve purchased a term plan with a given policy tenure, it is fixed and cannot be extended.
Also, here’s another thing that you should keep in mind. Once you’ve purchased a term insurance plan, ensure to make the premium payments on time. Failure to pay within the stipulated due date can cause your term insurance plan to lapse, which can also leave you and your family unprotected from the uncertainties of life.
